one_line_takeaway: LCC-LLM/LCCD pairs a large code-centric, CTI-enriched PE malware dataset with a RAG+verification-gated LLM framework, but the paper's own numbers show the fine-tuned model achieves good semantic fluency (0.634 mean similarity) while failing at exact factual grounding on the tasks that matter most for attribution (0% exact family-classification accuracy, 0.0 CWE F1, 0.426 F1 on MITRE technique mapping).
key_finding: CPT reduced train/eval loss from 2.89 to 0.92 and raised token accuracy from ~0.61 to over 0.80 over 4,200 steps; SFT reduced loss 87% (1.178 to 0.147) and raised token accuracy to 0.954 (eval accuracy 0.9537) over 1,550 steps, with output entropy dropping from 0.945 to 0.154. Across 43 task types the mean semantic similarity was 0.634. Critically, semantic similarity and exact-match factual correctness diverged sharply on the 3 tasks given supplementary metrics: MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping scored 0.761 similarity but only F1=0.426 (precision 0.526, recall 0.357) on exact technique-ID matching; Malware Family Classification scored 0.655 similarity but 0.0% exact accuracy (the model defaulted to the generic label 'classification' rather than a specific family name); Vulnerable Code Detection scored 0.506 similarity but CWE F1=0.0. In a MalwareBazaar case study the grounded pipeline achieved a 10/10 structured-analysis pass rate (producing CFG/FCG evidence, MITRE mappings, detection guidance, analyst-ready reports), though the paper does not detail pass/fail criteria for this metric in the extracted text. Qualitative 'complex scenario' tests showed the CoT/CoVe-trained model reasoning step-by-step but frequently hitting the token limit and truncating final structured output.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (distinct from the related-work survey in Section 2) is twofold: (1) LCCD, a Large-Scale Code-Centric Dataset of ~34,692 Windows PE samples (33,710 malicious, 982 benign) drawn from DikeDataset and MalwareBazaar (submissions Jan 2020-Jan 2026), each represented via a unified RetDec+Radare2 decompilation pipeline as decompiled C code, assembly, CFG/FCG (DOT + rasterized SVG), hex dumps, PE metadata, suspicious-API evidence, malicious/vulnerable code segment annotations (CWE-mapped), and CTI enrichment (MITRE ATT&CK, AlienVault OTX, MISP); and (2) LCC-LLM, an evidence-grounded inference framework that orchestrates static-analysis tool chains, a seven-layer hybrid (BM25 + dense + reciprocal-rank-fusion + cross-encoder rerank) RAG pipeline over four cybersecurity knowledge bases, a three-stage Architect-Analyst-Judge prompt-engineering pipeline (used to generate the 183,070 instruction-tuning pairs), Chain-of-Thought and Chain-of-Verification augmentation, and a Verification-Guided output-validation stage (Algorithm 4) that extracts security indicators (MITRE, CVE, CWE, CAPEC, CVSS, IP, hash, URL, email, port) and labels each as verified/valid-unverified/invalid before a weighted quality gate accepts, regenerates, or template-reconstructs the response. The authors fine-tune DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B via QLoRA (CPT on ~4,000 raw samples then SFT on 8,000 instruction triplets, sampled from the larger LCCD corpus) and evaluate it, plus separately mention Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B in the abstract as a second target model (the fine-tuning/results section in the body, however, only reports experiments for DeepSeek-R1-Distill-Qwen-14B; no Qwen3-Coder-30B-A3B results table appears in the first 22 pages of extracted text).
threat_model: This is a defensive/analyst-tooling paper, not an attack paper: there is no adversary model against the LLM system itself. The 'threat' addressed is analyst-facing unreliability -- LLM-based malware analysis producing hallucinated IoCs, unsupported family/attribution claims, and incorrect MITRE ATT&CK mappings that could mislead incident response. The paper's mitigation is evidentiary grounding (deterministic reverse-engineering tool output + retrieved CTI) plus a Chain-of-Verification-style indicator-provenance check, not adversarial robustness against malware authors evading the classifier or prompt-injecting the pipeline.
limitations: Explicitly acknowledged by the authors: (1) exact-match/entity-extraction performance is weak despite strong semantic similarity -- the model 'consistently outputted the generic term classification rather than accurately identifying specific family names' and needs 'more structured CWE training data'; (2) CoT/CoVe verbosity caused token-limit truncation of final outputs during complex-scenario inference, requiring either expanded context windows or brevity constraints; (3) training used only a 4,000-sample CPT subset and an 8,000-triplet SFT subset of the much larger LCCD corpus due to compute constraints, which the authors say may 'incrementally impact peak accuracy'; (4) decompilation yield is imperfect -- RetDec succeeded on 93.84% of samples (Radare2 99.95%), with obfuscated/packed binaries causing attrition; (5) the dataset retains real-world class imbalance (e.g., Rogueware category is 100% one family, Pielou's evenness 0.003) which the authors call intentional but which limits family-level generalization; (6) safety-guardrail refusals during LLM-based dataset generation required detection/discard/reprompting, an artifact of building malware-analysis training data with general-purpose LLMs.

datasets_used: MalwareBazaar (source of PE samples, Jan 2020-Jan 2026 submissions)
malpe_dp / 'Malware Detection PE-Based Analysis Using Deep Learning Algorithm Dataset' (ground-truth labels for DikeDataset subset)
Fenrir v2.0 (external cybersecurity instruction-tuning dataset used in SFT)
Trendyol Cybersecurity Defense Instruction-Tuning Dataset v2.0 (external, used in SFT)
CVE Chat-Style Multi-Turn Cybersecurity Dataset (external, used in SFT)
quantitative_results: Radare2 processing success rate 99.95%; RetDec 93.84%; both tools successful 93.79%; complete base embeddings 92.25%
183,070 instruction-tuning sample-prompt pairs across 12 task types
CPT: train/eval loss 2.89 -> 0.92 over 4,200 steps; token accuracy 0.61 -> 0.80; final train accuracy 0.8027
SFT: loss reduced 87% (1.178 -> 0.147) over 1,550 steps; token accuracy 0.740 -> 0.954; final eval accuracy 0.9537; output entropy 0.945 -> 0.154
Mean semantic similarity across 43 evaluated task types: 0.634
MITRE ATT&CK technique mapping: similarity 0.761, precision 0.526, recall 0.357, F1 0.426
Malware Family Classification: similarity 0.655, exact accuracy 0.0%
Vulnerable Code Detection: similarity 0.506, CWE F1 0.0
MalwareBazaar case study: 10/10 structured-analysis pass rate
Training infra: 1 node x 4 NVIDIA A100 80GB GPUs, 300GB RAM, 32 CPU cores
baselines_compared: No external baseline model or competing malware-attribution system is benchmarked against LCC-LLM in the extracted text; comparisons made are (a) qualitative dataset-feature comparisons against prior malware datasets (Malicia, Microsoft BIG-2015, EMBER 2018, SOREL-20M, BODMAS, CIC-SGG-2024, FCG-MFD, SBAN) in Table 1, and (b) an internal comparison between the paper's own semantic-similarity metric and its own exact-match metrics on the same model's outputs (Table 8), not a comparison against a different system or prior LLM baseline.
